title: How to Revoke an S-Corp Election

interlinking_suggestions.title: Sole Proprietorship vs. LLC: The Definitive Guide for Global Freelancers
A Guide to Converting an LLC to an S-Corp

author.name: Ben Carter
author.bio: A certified financial planner specializing in the unique challenges faced by US citizens abroad. Ben's articles provide actionable advice on everything from FBAR and FATCA compliance to retirement planning for expats.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Revoke your S-Corp status to become eligible for venture capital, private equity, or foreign investment, as its shareholder restrictions legally prohibit these funding sources.

If you are a U.S. citizen living abroad, evaluate revoking your S-Corp as its pass-through profits often do not qualify for the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE), creating a significant tax liability.
Proactively convert to a C-Corporation well before a potential sale, as this is the preferred structure for large corporate acquirers and can increase your company's valuation.
Treat the decision to revoke as a five-year commitment, as IRS rules generally prevent you from re-electing S-Corp status for that duration, demanding absolute strategic certainty.
Immediately after revoking, you must switch from a distribution-focused compensation model to a formal W-2 salary to avoid the double taxation inherent in C-Corp dividends.
summary: The S-Corp structure creates a strategic ceiling for ambitious businesses, as its rigid shareholder rules block access to institutional capital and can create tax traps for global professionals. The article advises proactively revoking this status through a precise legal process, which involves securing shareholder consent and filing a formal statement with the IRS before the March 15th deadline. This strategic pivot realigns the company's architecture for growth, unlocking the ability to accept venture capital, optimize for an international lifestyle, and position the business for a more attractive strategic exit.
ai_overview_answer: Revoking an S-Corp election is a strategic decision made when a business needs to attract venture capital or foreign investors, optimize taxes for owners living abroad, or prepare for a strategic sale to a buyer who prefers a C-Corporation structure. The process requires obtaining formal, documented consent from shareholders who hold more than 50% of the company's stock. Since the IRS does not provide a specific form, you must draft a formal revocation statement containing all required corporate and shareholder details. To make the revocation effective for the entire current tax year, this statement must be filed by March 15th. After this deadline, the revocation generally becomes effective at the start of the next tax year. Be aware that after revoking, a company is typically prohibited from re-electing S-Corp status for five years. Upon revocation, a corporation defaults to a C-Corp, while an LLC reverts to its original tax status as either a partnership or a disregarded entity. This change necessitates an immediate shift in financial operations, particularly adjusting compensation from a distribution-focused model to a reasonable salary structure to manage the new tax implications.

@graph.description: Learn when and how to revoke an S-Corp election to unlock global capital, prepare for a strategic exit, or optimize taxes. Get the step-by-step professional guide.

step.name: Secure and Document Shareholder Consent
step.text: Before notifying the IRS, you must formalize the decision internally. The revocation requires consent from shareholders holding more than 50% of the company's stock, including both voting and non-voting shares. Document this approval in a signed Shareholder Consent Resolution.

step.name: Draft the Formal Revocation Statement
step.text: The IRS does not provide a specific form for this process. You must draft a formal revocation statement yourself containing clear intent, corporate details, shareholder information, stock details, the effective date, and authorized signatures.

step.name: Ensure Secure Filing and Proof of Delivery
step.text: To create an irrefutable record of a timely filing, send the complete package to the IRS service center where you file your annual return using Certified Mail with Return Receipt. This provides legal proof of the mailing date and a signature confirmation.

step.name: Navigate the Critical March 15th Deadline
step.text: To make the change effective for the entire current tax year (retroactive to January 1st), you must file your statement by the 16th day of the third month of that tax year—typically March 15th. If you file after this deadline, the revocation generally becomes effective on the first day of the next taxable year.

step.name: Acknowledge Your New Tax Identity
step.text: The moment your revocation is effective, your business has a new default tax status. For a corporation, you are now a C-Corporation. For an LLC, it reverts to its default classification (Partnership or Disregarded Entity).

step.name: Overhaul Your Financial Cadence
step.text: Switch from the S-Corp strategy of taking a modest salary and large distributions to a C-Corp model. The primary way to extract profit without double taxation is by paying yourself a reasonable W-2 salary, which is a deductible business expense.

step.name: Complete the Post-Revocation Compliance Checklist
step.text: Notify state taxing authorities of the change, update your entity's tax classification in your accounting software, alert your payroll provider, and consult your tax advisor to model your new total tax liability.

mainEntity.name: What is the deadline to revoke S-Corp status for the current tax year?

acceptedAnswer.text: For a calendar-year business, the revocation statement must be filed by March 15th to be effective retroactively to January 1st of that year. If filed after that date, the revocation takes effect on the first day of the *next* tax year, unless you specify a different prospective date.

mainEntity.name: Can you retroactively revoke an S-Corp election for a prior year?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. The IRS does not permit revoking an S-Corp election for a tax year that has already ended. The only retroactive provision is the one allowing a revocation filed by March 15th to apply to the current year. All decisions must be forward-looking.

mainEntity.name: Who must approve an S-Corp revocation?

acceptedAnswer.text: A voluntary revocation requires formal consent from shareholders holding more than 50% of the corporation's total outstanding stock, including both voting and non-voting shares. This consent must be documented in a formal resolution.

mainEntity.name: What tax form is used to revoke an S-Corp election?

acceptedAnswer.text: There is no specific numbered IRS form. You must submit a formal, signed revocation statement—essentially a detailed letter—to the IRS containing all required corporate and shareholder information, along with the effective date.

mainEntity.name: What happens to an LLC after revoking its S-Corp election?

acceptedAnswer.text: An LLC reverts to its default IRS tax classification. A multi-member LLC reverts to being taxed as a Partnership, while a single-member LLC reverts to a "disregarded entity" (taxed as a sole proprietorship). It does not automatically become a C-Corporation.

mainEntity.name: How long must I wait to become an S-Corp again?

acceptedAnswer.text: Due to the five-year rule, a corporation that revokes its S-Corp status must generally wait five taxable years before it can re-elect. This makes the decision a significant long-term commitment that should be made with strategic certainty.

body.content: The Decision Matrix: When Revoking Your S-Corp Becomes a Strategic Imperative

The decision to revoke an S-Corp election is not a compliance task; it is a strategic pivot. For the elite global professional, it signals a deliberate shift away from a structure designed for simplicity toward one built for scale, international reach, and high-value exits. This is not about paperwork—it is about aligning your corporate architecture with the full scope of your ambition.
This guide moves beyond mere procedure to focus on the critical triggers for revocation, the flawless execution of the process, and the essential first steps in your new operational reality.
Strategic Triggers: When Revocation Becomes Necessary

An S-Corp election should be revoked not as a reaction, but as a proactive move to unlock future opportunities. For the global professional, several distinct scenarios elevate this decision from a theoretical option to an absolute necessity.

items: **Unlocking Global Capital:** The S-Corp's primary virtue—simplicity—is also its greatest limitation. Its rigid shareholder rules place a hard ceiling on growth. An S-Corp cannot have more than 100 shareholders, all of whom must be U.S. citizens or resident aliens. This immediately disqualifies your business from serious funding rounds involving venture capital, private equity, or foreign investors, as these entities are legally prohibited from owning your stock. If your roadmap requires outside capital to scale, revoking your S-Corp status is the mandatory first step to open the door to transformative investment.

**Optimizing for a Global Lifestyle:** Your business structure must serve your life, not constrain it. For a U.S. citizen living abroad, the S-Corp can become a significant tax liability. While the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) is a powerful tool for expatriates, it applies only to *earned* income, such as a salary. The profits that pass through an S-Corp to you are often treated as unearned income and are therefore not eligible for the exclusion. This creates a tax trap where a structure that was beneficial in the U.S. undermines your financial strategy abroad. Revocation allows you to adopt a structure, typically a C-Corp, that is better suited to a global financial footprint.

**Preparing for a Strategic Exit:** When positioning your company for sale, the acquirer's preferences are paramount. Large corporate buyers and private equity firms overwhelmingly prefer to purchase C-Corporations. The reasons are structural: C-Corps have no ownership restrictions, can issue multiple classes of stock to facilitate complex deals, and allow for simpler, tax-advantaged reorganizations. Anticipating this preference and converting to a C-Corp well in advance can remove significant friction from the M&A process, making your company a more attractive target and potentially increasing its valuation.

**Understanding the Five-Year Rule:** Revoking your S-Corp status is, for all practical purposes, a one-way door for half a decade. The IRS generally prohibits a company from re-electing S-Corp status for five taxable years after a revocation takes effect. This rule forces strategic clarity. You must be certain that your long-term vision—whether it requires institutional funding, global mobility, or a streamlined exit—justifies a permanent departure from the S-Corp structure.

body.content: The Revocation Process: A Step-by-Step Guide for Flawless Execution

Once the strategic decision is made, the focus shifts from "why" to a flawless "how." A misstep in the IRS procedure can lead to costly delays or rejection. This methodical process ensures your revocation is accepted without issue, giving you complete control over your corporate transition.
Step 1: Secure and Document Shareholder Consent

Before notifying the IRS, you must formalize the decision internally. The revocation requires consent from shareholders holding **more than 50% of the company's stock**, including both voting and non-voting shares. This is a formal corporate act, not a casual conversation.

Document this approval in a signed **Shareholder Consent Resolution**. This internal document is a critical piece of your corporate records, providing a clear paper trail that protects you from future disputes and demonstrates proper governance.
Step 2: Draft the Formal Revocation Statement
The IRS does not provide a specific form for this process. You must draft a formal revocation statement yourself. To avoid rejection, your statement must contain the following key information:

rows: A direct sentence stating the corporation is revoking its election under Internal Revenue Code Section 1362(a).
The full legal name of the corporation, its address, and its Employer Identification Number (EIN).
Names, addresses, and taxpayer ID numbers for each consenting shareholder.
The total number of shares issued and outstanding. For each consenting shareholder, list the number of shares they own.
Clearly state the exact date you want the revocation to be effective.
The statement must be signed by a corporate officer authorized to sign the tax return and by each consenting shareholder, under penalty of perjury.

body.content: Attach the signed Shareholder Consent Resolution from Step 1 to this formal statement.
Step 3: Ensure Secure Filing and Proof of Delivery

How you send your revocation is as important as what it contains. To create an irrefutable record of a timely filing, send the complete package to the IRS service center where you file your annual return using **Certified Mail with Return Receipt**. This service provides a postmarked receipt—legal proof of the mailing date—and a signature confirmation when the IRS receives it. This simple step eliminates any anxiety about missed deadlines or lost mail.
Step 4: Navigate the Critical March 15th Deadline

Timing dictates the effective date of your revocation. To make the change effective for the *entire current tax year* (retroactive to January 1st for calendar-year businesses), you must file your statement by the 16th day of the third month of that tax year—typically **March 15th**.

items: **If you file after this deadline,** the revocation generally becomes effective on the first day of the *next* taxable year.

**Alternatively, you can specify a future effective date.** You may file at any point during the year and designate a prospective date, as long as it is on or after the day you file. This allows for precise tax planning and absolute control over your transition.

body.content: The Transition: Your First 90 Days After Revocation
Filing the paperwork is not the final move—it is the beginning of a new operational reality. The following 90-day plan ensures your strategic decision translates into seamless execution.
Day 1: Acknowledge Your New Tax Identity
The moment your revocation is effective, your business has a new default tax status, fundamentally altering how profits are taxed.

items: **For an entity legally formed as a corporation:** You are now, by default, a **C-Corporation**. It is a separate taxpaying entity that files its own return (Form 1120) and pays tax at the corporate level.

**For a Limited Liability Company (LLC):** An LLC that revokes its S-Corp election reverts to its *default* tax classification. A multi-member LLC becomes a Partnership (filing Form 1065), and a single-member LLC becomes a Disregarded Entity (reported on your personal tax return). To avoid ambiguity, it is best practice to file Form 8832, *Entity Classification Election*, to formally declare your intended post-revocation status to the IRS.

body.content: Day 1-30: Overhaul Your Financial Cadence
Your old compensation habits are now a liability. The S-Corp strategy of taking a modest salary and large, tax-advantaged distributions is over.

The most significant mistake owners make post-revocation is failing to switch from a distribution-focused model to a reasonable salary structure. They continue taking owner draws, not realizing those are now non-deductible dividends from a C-Corp, leading to double taxation.
body.author: Brent SaGres, JD, LL.M., Partner at SaGres & Associates

body.content: In a C-Corp, profits are taxed once at the corporate level and again at the personal level when distributed as dividends. To manage this, you must immediately adjust your compensation.

rows: Salary + Distributions: Low salary to minimize payroll taxes; profits taken as tax-advantaged distributions.
Salary vs. Dividends: Payments are either a W-2 salary (a deductible business expense) or a dividend (a non-deductible distribution).
The primary way to extract profit without double taxation is by paying yourself a reasonable salary.
Personal Estimated Taxes: Paid on all pass-through income.
Corporate & Personal Estimated Taxes: The C-Corp pays its own estimated taxes. You pay personal estimates on your salary and dividends.
Your tax payment process is now split into two separate streams: corporate and personal.

body.content: Day 30-60: Complete the Post-Revocation Compliance Checklist
Your duty to inform extends beyond the IRS. Update key stakeholders and platforms to align with your new structure.

items: **Notify State Taxing Authorities:** If your state recognized your S-Corp election, you must formally notify them of the revocation.
**Update Accounting Software:** Change your entity's tax classification in your accounting platform to ensure accurate financial reports and tax projections.
**Alert Your Payroll Provider:** Inform your payroll provider of the change to ensure correct tax withholding on your W-2 salary.
**Consult Your Tax Advisor:** Schedule a meeting to model your new total tax liability, including corporate tax and your personal income tax on salary and potential dividends.

body.content: Frequently Asked Questions
**What is the deadline to revoke S-Corp status for the current tax year?**

For a calendar-year business, the revocation statement must be filed by March 15th to be effective retroactively to January 1st of that year. If filed after that date, the revocation takes effect on the first day of the *next* tax year, unless you specify a different prospective date.
**Can you retroactively revoke an S-Corp election for a prior year?**

No. The IRS does not permit revoking an S-Corp election for a tax year that has already ended. The only retroactive provision is the one allowing a revocation filed by March 15th to apply to the current year. All decisions must be forward-looking.
**Who must approve an S-Corp revocation?**

A voluntary revocation requires formal consent from shareholders holding more than 50% of the corporation's total outstanding stock, including both voting and non-voting shares. This consent must be documented in a formal resolution.
**What tax form is used to revoke an S-Corp election?**

There is no specific numbered IRS form. You must submit a formal, signed revocation statement—essentially a detailed letter—to the IRS containing all required corporate and shareholder information, along with the effective date.
**What happens to an LLC after revoking its S-Corp election?**

An LLC reverts to its default IRS tax classification. A multi-member LLC reverts to being taxed as a Partnership, while a single-member LLC reverts to a "disregarded entity" (taxed as a sole proprietorship). It does not automatically become a C-Corporation.
**How long must I wait to become an S-Corp again?**

Due to the five-year rule, a corporation that revokes its S-Corp status must generally wait five taxable years before it can re-elect. This makes the decision a significant long-term commitment that should be made with strategic certainty.
Conclusion: Revocation as the Ultimate Act of Strategic Control

Viewing the S-Corp revocation purely through a lens of compliance is a mistake. It is not about avoiding penalties; it is about seizing control. The choice to revoke your S-Corp election is one of the most powerful strategic levers you can pull to shape the future of your enterprise, signaling a deliberate shift from a domestic-focused structure to one engineered for global ambition.

A reactive owner sees corporate structure as a box. A strategic architect understands it must be engineered to support the weight of their goals. The S-Corp’s limitations—its cap on shareholders and prohibition of foreign or corporate investors—are foundational weaknesses when your objective is to attract venture capital or position for a strategic acquisition. Proactively revoking your S-Corp status is not a reaction to a term sheet; it is the deliberate preparation of your company’s foundation to welcome that level of opportunity.

This decision is a declaration of intent. It is a forward-looking move that anticipates future needs for capital, prepares your entity for a lucrative exit, or aligns your corporate finances with the realities of a life lived across borders. By wielding corporate governance as a competitive advantage, you are no longer just a business owner following rules; you are the architect of your enterprise, making informed, confident decisions that empower your global future.
